speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number five. The survivor is Mania Lichtenstein. Today is April 1, 1996. Before we continue further, do you recall the names of the German leaders of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the name Westingcheide, something. Maybe I'm saying, Westingcheide. He was the-- the big shot, the one in charge of this operation. And there was the [NON-ENGLISH] Commissaire [? Krauser. ?] He was like under him, who took care of more details. He was the Commissaire in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no, no. German. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a top Ukrainian--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in charge, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they call him? Ivan the-- Ivan the [NON-ENGLISH]. How do you say? Ivan the, what do you call it? Oh, gosh. Because you say-- you say in English, you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ivan the Terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Terrible, yeah. [NON-ENGLISH], yeah. [LAUGHS] Ivan the-- Ivan [NON-ENGLISH], Ivan the Terrible. He was a cold-blooded murderer who did more than it was necessary, you know? With pleasure, as they say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you personally see any of these three people?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I do not remember if I saw him. I really-- certain things I can't recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We're talking now about the second ghetto, when you were placed with four other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Five other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were six, altogether.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- in a little cubicle. It didn't matter. I don't even remember how-- It didn't matter how you slept, what you did. You just-- They make you exist a little longer.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you together with the five other girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: It took two months until the following program-- pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have to eat or drink during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, there was a kitchen. Because we didn't have, really-- There was no source of where you can go, and-- or even a garden, and pick something. I don't know. I really-- sometimes I think, what did we eat, what did we do? I don't know. But, luckily, it was only two months. We had a kitchen that gave us coffee. I suppose we didn't need to eat much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in this one. I sang with my sisters because we were a very musical family, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you dream? Did you have any dreams while you were in the ghetto, the second ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? You didn't have time for it. It's not when you-- Still, like in the first ghetto, they didn't kill you yet. You refused to believe. You were still-- You did not experience it. You heard that things like this happen, but, oh, no. Not to me, you know? Why should it-- Why should it be?

speaker: interviewer
text: You afford dreams, but there, there was no-- no-- I don't know. As I say, no laughing, no crying, no dreaming. There was no, too-- too big luxuries, you know? So they were taking us out for work, which was, in a way, good. They took us for work. Again, lifting heavy debris. Actually, I was such a big worker. You know. But, they make me one. So I lift a frame, a window frame, from a bumped-out house. Or maybe they destroyed it? And that frame goes right into my-- I still have a-- a scar here-- right into my-- lucky, not into my eye, but on my brow. Then I start bleeding, and they let me go home. They let me go home. Home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was-- Home, oh, god. To the ghetto. Back to the ghetto because I was useless, already. I was bleeding, and I remember my friends had pity on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, you know, oh, when we saw blood, we thought that-- We were so protected, kind of. Nowadays, it's different. You see so much gore that, even shooting somebody is no big deal, you know? It's a different-- But we were so shielded from such gory things, you know? And they found some-- a tomato, no, a potato, I remember, and we had a potato. I don't know where they find-- It's like, they were so happy to see me, like they-- out of pity, because I was bleeding. Oh, you know? You were bleeding, you were so, oh, my God. How did they take care of your bleeding? How did you make it stop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess it just stopped. Nobody took care of anything. You know, it's amazing. A necessity, you just-- Like, I saw a program yesterday on TV, the man who would-- His wife, who lived in Africa somewhere with the lions. And I was thinking, what if he gets sick? What if he-- he cuts himself? But when you-- you just-- Nature takes care of everything, if it-- if it has to. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the names of these five other girls that you were staying with?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some. Gosh. One was [? Poppa ?] [? Fine. ?] There was [? Genya ?] [? Schaeffer. ?] [? Chanka, ?] I forgot her, [? Chanka. ?] Who else was there? I can't remember. I can't see why I can't remember these names. I stayed with them, you know, day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- And they were all alone, left, you know? Everybody of their family was killed, you know. By the second program-- pogrom, there were only young people left. Did you have any hope at all, that you were going to live?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] No. I don't know. It didn't even take place, for a minute, in my mind. It's just to get over. They were so lucky. They were already-- It's quiet, now. It's done. It's quiet. Almost nice, you know? As a matter of fact, after the war, we lived in Hamburg. Excuse me, in Montreal. I lived in so many places that sometimes, when I go away I say, oh, I left Hamburg or I left Montreal. And there was an older lady, like one of my friends who came from Siberia, and I would tell her, I'd say, I was so glad that my parents were gone. And she-- she couldn't forgive me for it. And I didn't mean to be mean, but I knew, if my mother had survived, and I and my sisters, we are all gone, it would be hell for her. I knew-- I knew how she would feel. I knew what was happening to her in the ghetto, before it was happening. But just knowing that, here is my precious family that I brought up, and I know what's going to happen to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it's not just, you know, happen, where they put the bullet in-- in-- in your head, you know? It's terrible for a mother to-- to feel. You know-- myself? OK, whatever happens. So after a few months, living together with these five other girls.

speaker: interviewee
text: Two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two months, I'm  sorry, with these other young women, I should say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, predictions. The 15th, the 20th, which didn't take place. There was always, maybe leaking, maybe imagining, maybe somebody start-- starting a rumor. On the 15th, there's gonna be the pogrom.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month was this? Let's try to place this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was November.

speaker: interviewer
text: November--

speaker: interviewee
text: November, '42, when we entered the second ghetto. And two months later, it was eliminated. The pogrom, it was eliminated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're sometime in November or December of '42?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, November-- no till December. November and December.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were hearing the rumors about which date.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a rumor. But, one day, the rumor came true. They said that pogrom is going to take place. What-- what can you do? You wear your coat, and you'll sit and wait. But, there was one couple I knew. Not before the war, but during all this doings. And they were in that other ghetto, and I was going to work there, in that other ghetto, in the living ghetto. At that time, again they-- again, they maintained a living and a dead. And again, the living, you need a piece of paper showing that-- what you-- who you are. And the dead, is dead. And I was in the dead ghetto, and the pogrom was predicted for next morning. And she said, that was Chanka Oks and her husband. They live in New York, now. Hopefully, they still alive. And her husband, Wigdor. Wigdor. And she says, come-- come overnight. I know we don't-- We knew what happened to the living ghetto, so we-- it didn't mean anything to us. We didn't believe in the trick anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first time, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she says, who knows? They'll kill you here. They'll kill you there. Come. And they knew that I was hungry. And, I don't know, the Polish-- Jewish-Polish mentality? You don't admit you're hungry. I don't know why it was so-- To be poor, you know? I mean, this case, you weren't poor, you just didn't have food.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I wouldn't admit that food-- Oh, give me food, and I don't care what happened. Well, she maybe says-- And see, she had a husband, so she-- You cared more how to obtain this food. If I had a sister with me, I would too, go and dig up, I don't know what? I'll produce something. But myself, I didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, come, I'll-- I'll cook something. She says, I'll make something and I figure, OK, I'll go. And this is what saved me from that second pogrom. But immediately, next morning, the predictions came true.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, can I just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --try to place-- This is the second ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is the-- They divided the second ghetto, again, into a living section?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Two separate places, kind of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it divided? Physically divided?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physically divided, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: By-- by what? By barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Because without the barbed wire, you might sneak out. I don't know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did they decide who was going to be in one ghetto, or the other one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only who could produce-- And you had-- you couldn't produce just saying you are, if you were not. All the shoe makers, all the tailors, all the candle makers, all the whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So because you didn't have a profession--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't have a profession, so I belonged in the dead ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: But she persuaded me to come, which I consented to.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from one side to the other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but I-- I was there, that was the day before, and I was supposed to work there. You know, like they took us to work, and she says, stay here. Because they been supervising us. They knew that no matter, we have no way-- nowhere to run, you know? And we have to go back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Who shall I go to? To a Pole, to a Ukrainian? Who's gonna take me in? This was my home. So this-- the day before I came to her and I slept over. And sure enough, next morning, the second pogrom took place. Now, they were supposed to kill only 6,000 this time. Now, 6,000 is a-- nothing to them, you know? It was done in no time, within a few hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I know because-- Oh, god. I wish I can tell you a happy story. I have no happy stories, here. So the ghetto, within a few hours, the dead ghetto, all my little friends there, that live with me, everything is gone, and I survived. Again, fate. I hadn't consented to go and sleep over. At that point, I mean, if this wasn't my fate to be, already. But, in the meantime, for the time being, I'm still alive, and I'm in the living ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the other people being rounded up and taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I wasn't-- But, I'll tell you how I knew. Immediately-- we were put to so many uses. Immediately, the living ghetto-- Now, we are living ghetto, we are living people, now. 1,000 were left in that living ghetto. There might be-- There might have been more, but if they didn't have a piece of paper, they were hiding in a cellar, in a-- in any hole possible, because they used to-- I'll-- I'll get to it later, what was happening to them. I'm in this ghetto, and immediately they, of course, through the Judenrat. Hey, we need so many women or men to bring to the red school, it was called. It was a red building, and they called it the red school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, they brought from the graves, all the clothing from these 6,000. What happened of the 19,000? I don't know what happened. Maybe-- I-- I'm not aware. But the 6,000--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am standing there. The big tables they put up, and we sort. Goes to Germany, sweaters, blouses, coats. You know, whatever. All the clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothing are hot, they're warm. I put my hand into a man's cap, and my whole hand is bloody because they must have shot him, and-- but they didn't realize. My hand comes out, but I-- I just have to see blood. But at that time, blood, live, dead. I don't know? It was just, without any senses. Then I come across my grandmother's jacket. Warm. My-- And, you know, you knew. It's not like here. You-- You could have had the same jacket as I, you know? No. How did you know, for sure, that it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Oh, no. My god. And then my sister's sweater. When-- As I said, we unraveled, we didn't have new wool, but we had to do something, so we knitted, and we sang, and we cried. And my sister knitted such an intricate design, a sweater. Meantime, we knitted, you know. It was a piece of clothing. Beautiful. I can still draw it. I don't know how to do it, but-- [? Off of ?] the tree are multi-colored because whatever you had, you-- you combined. And here is my sister's sweaters-- sweater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Rifka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rifka, yeah. So I knew that was their clothing. I knew my-- my grandmother's jacket. I knew it well. It's not that you had so many that, no. So we are sorting all this stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your reaction, when you saw it?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what? I didn't cry because they were the lucky one. I don't know. I-- I'm doubting myself, now. Was I right, to think like that? I don't know if I was right or wrong. But they had peace. I still have to do it, so they-- they-- they in paradise. They in paradise. But still, my sister's sweaters that I remember, when she was knitting it. My grandmother. My-- my-- my wonderful grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we work. We sort, because all this stuff has to go. Germany wasn't in such a good economical situation, there, you know? The-- That's why Hitler was able to-- to raise their spirits and promise them such good thing. You were aware of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they needed everything they could-- everything-- They probably even send out all the furniture. Everything that the Jews left behind, and all the clothing. I mean, that all went to Germany. And, that was the end of the ghetto, and the end of the clothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what I remember is, it was warm because there was heaps. Can you imagine? Even-- even 6,000 is-- is quite a number, right? And-- and-- and the clothes were so warm, still. Just from the dead, because they were taken the same thing at the first pogrom. Undress naked. This is why I-- my sleepless nights, because suddenly everything is so graphic. Suddenly I see and I hear, you know? Before, in the earlier years, I-- I kept myself from thinking. I knew it happened. How? No idea. Like, I-- I forbid myself to-- to be graphic in my mind. Now, it's graphic. It's graphic, and it's audible. And I can see how they round the-- the graves. They see the graves. And-- and-- and women, men, children. At that time, and in those days, you were especially, very modest, you know? To run naked and-- and-- and then children crying, and babies. You know/

speaker: interviewee
text: And you have to jump, yet, in the grave, and then be shot. Which-- which again, I know exactly. Not from hearing because on one of the-- [SIGHS] one of the-- the work, the forced labor-- oh, my mind is getting-- Forced labor, there was that German. His name was Keller. Herr Keller. Mr. Keller. Drunk, they were all drunk. You know, having a good time. And also, it was-- it helped to cope with everything, easier.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and as we are doing the work in that bombed-out house, he is sitting and grinning. And he says, I killed-- What did he say? 10,000 Jews I shot. And he was getting 30 [NON-ENGLISH], let's say $0.30, for each head.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know. So he shot. I know, I know because I'll tell you later, again, when we get to it, how I know. I saw it with my two eyes, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they undressed naked, leave the clothes behind, because they better make use of it, the Germans, and jump with your children. To see your own children jumping. Yes, jumping, not just standing and shoot me. No, you have to jump in this grave, one on top of the other. You're alive. You're dead. It didn't matter. And, like he was saying, da, da, da, da, shooting like with the-- with the rifle, automatic rifle. Da, da, da, da, da. Then another layer, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't matter if you were still alive, or if you're shot only in your arm. You were there, and that's it. As I say here, the others drowned slowly. Lucky were those quickly shot and dead. The others drowned slowly in a blood-filled bed. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it had to be done quick and, you know, get over it, for them. It wasn't for them, their favorite occupation, too. You have to do it and get over with it. So all-- all is gone and now a new ghetto is-- is arising. I mean, the living ghetto where we were, there. Only 1,000. As I say, some didn't have that piece of it. Like my late husband and brother-in-law didn't have that piece of paper, so they were-- but they were alive. They were in this ghetto, you know, so they were hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, sporadically, every so often, the Germans or Ukrainians would ambush the ghetto and check who doesn't have the piece of paper. If you didn't have the piece of paper, out you went, and you were shot because you were not allowed to be here. So most of the time, they were hiding in that last little ghetto. I, too, didn't have a piece of paper, but as it happened, there was that Czechoslovakian Jew, Leon Hirschhorn from Czechoslovakia. I don't know how he happened to find himself in our-- in Poland, in our part. He was a candle maker, and he was entitled to stay in that ghetto. He had the piece of paper that says [NON-ENGLISH], candle maker, and he's eligible to remain. So did his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one morning she ventured out, and she did not have that piece of paper with her, and they ambushed. Because they did it like, ambush the ghetto, show your papers. You had to have this piece of paper because, what do they care? In the meantime, they shoot you if you tell them you have it at home. Maybe you're lying. So, in the meantime, it didn't matter. Another one gone. And they shot her. She was pleading, she has a piece of paper at home, but what did they care? She was shot. And that man had that piece of paper. Now, somebody I knew, knew him, and he said, I'm going to ask him, you have it? It did not, really-- You see, the name was already Sarah Hirschhorn, which didn't matter. I didn't care what name I have, or what-- what year I was born. It didn't matter. But she was born in 1906. And, you know, when you're young, and such a discrepancy in ages. I mean, when you're 15 and 18, you can get away with. But and especially, I was frail, you know? I-- I'm petite and I'm-- I was frail. I was more, never looked my age. Like, rather childish looking. How can I be 1906? So what I did is, I erased a zero and put a one on top of the zero. Which, if you come and look at it closely, you can tell that it was tampered. I was afraid, already, to do more, you know? That would be really-- So I figure I'll carry that piece of paper. Hopefully, they'll never get to stop me. In the meantime, I still have, like I had that [NON-ENGLISH]. It was called [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. That means, working shop, working shops. You don't say [NON-ENGLISH] in the English? [NON-ENGLISH], it's French.

speaker: interviewee
text: The working whatever, working place. And [NON-ENGLISH] means, the document. And it says the city, and the date, and your name, and all this, and your-- your-- your trade.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did this gentleman decide to give it to you, instead of somebody else? Did you have any special--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, it's-- it's because that man knew-- knew-- knew him.

speaker: interviewer
text: A friend?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and he says, you know, they shot his wife, and he has this piece of paper. It's not that I would parade with it because it was so obvious, it cannot be me. But, it was better than nothing. So I existed. Immediately, of course, you were supposed to live only because you are-- you have a trade. So they're were about 15 young, I would say 15, I don't-- 15, more than 10, young women in that room, knitting all day long for the Germans and their Polish girlfriends. So you know, it was war and anything comes in handy. They brought wool, we knitted. We even knitted pants, ski pants for their girlfriends. You know. Anything was good, after all. It's Poland, and its way back, and it's-- and it's a war. We knitted caps and a lot of socks for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they give you to eat, during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I-- I don't-- I sometimes fight to remember. We-- we dreamt a lot about food, you know? And it's funny. Maybe one funny thing I'll jump in with. Everybody was dreaming of food. Real food. Oh, if we survive, if I survive, I'll have a roast beef. If I survive, they talk. And-- and my, this is how my mind only speeds. If I survive, I'll-- I'll eat so much cake and jam. [LAUGHS] And they said, yeah, if you survive and you get married-- At that time, it was customary to give the [NON-ENGLISH], dowry. We'll give you a big, if you survive, a big jar of jam they're gonna give me for my wedding. I say, I'll jump into the jar, and I'll-- I'll dwell in it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, I-- I-- I really don't know what? We ate very little. Whatever somebody could sneak in. Was there enough water to drink?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water, yes. Water, yes. Yeah, water, yes. I just remember my most favorite-- favorite-- The meal that I can't forget the taste, and I'm still searching for it. There were two boys, and they were taken to an oil refinery that was like in the outskirts of our town. And they sneaked out of that oil refinery, a little bottle of oil, and they gave us. Somewhere we found a tomato.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not tomatoes were good. I'm not much, like I eat it because you have to, you know? I'm not much, but our tomatoes-- Because it wasn't like here, that they are artificially ripened, you know? They were straight from the garden.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and the girls got up that tomato, some salt, and that oil that they brought, a little-- I think that this was the most heavenly taste. And I'm still, when I'm looking at tomatoes, I'm searching for a tomato that maybe would taste like the one.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the best tomato that you ever had.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was so heavenly. And I was not like a-- this kind of a eater. Like, I'd rather, I would talk about heavenly food, I'll talk about cake. You know? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a big piece of the tomato?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. I ate a slice. But we didn't have tomatoes, you know? So this was as far as food. Honestly, when I-- sometimes I say, hey, what did we do? What did we eat? It wasn't like breakfast, lunch, and dinner. This, I can assure you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were knitting the sweaters and clothes for the Germans and their girlfriends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, hoping that they'll never stop me, and I was a living entity in that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did that continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: This continued one year, one month, and one day, exactly, after the second pogrom. Yeah. I just always remember. Now, the exact date, I-- all I know is September 1. The first one, two months later. The second one, which again, two months. I don't know if it was, again, on the 1st or-- I don't. I won't try to rack my brains, even. It won't help, anyway. And then I remember, this I remember, that memory was acquired while I was right there. One year, one month, and one day.
